video_title: Why Everyone Is Buying Pokémon Cards Again
gist: Andy 8052 (Monster) argues the current Pokémon/collectibles boom is driven by 'gotcha' pack-opening platforms (RIPs, Arena Club, Courtyard, Collector Crypt) that move hundreds of millions of dollars a month and must constantly buy physical cards to replenish redeemed inventory, plus mid-30s/40s collectors with disposable income. He frames these repack platforms as a Trojan horse quietly bringing the collectibles market on-chain while warning the market may be at a local top.

insights.claim: Digital 'gotcha' repack platforms (RIPs, Arena Club, Courtyard, Collector Crypt, Figitals) collectively move hundreds of millions of dollars of pack-opening volume per month, making them a primary driver of the Pokémon card boom.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: The new marginal buyer of physical Pokémon cards is platform inventory demand, not end collectors — watch these platforms' volume as the leading indicator of card prices.
insights.quote: they're having hundreds of millions of dollars worth of of volume go through their platforms

insights.claim: Roughly 20-30% of the dollar volume run through a gotcha platform is redeemed (shipped) by users rather than sold back, forcing platforms to continually buy physical cards to replenish inventory and bidding up prices.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Redemption rates create persistent, price-insensitive physical card demand; a drop in redemption or volume would remove a structural bid under the market.
insights.quote: it's about about 30% of the volume played through, 20%, it depends on the day and the cards and all this is redeemed by users

insights.claim: The two largest pack-opening platforms, RIPs and Arena Club, use no crypto at all, while crypto-native players (Courtyard, Collector Crypt, Figitals) are smaller, meaning the biggest competition is off-chain.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: On-chain collectible platforms are fighting incumbents with far larger non-crypto distribution; market share, not just tokenization, is the real battle.
insights.quote: the biggest of these platforms are not crypto at all. The two biggest that exist are called RIPs and um Arena Club

insights.claim: Platforms like Monster price packs at a positive expected value (a $50 pack averages ~$55, or 102-103 cents on the dollar pulled), deliberately paying users to open packs and recouping margin on buybacks.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: The growth model subsidizes openers and depends on enough users selling cards back; understanding unit economics matters for assessing platform sustainability.
insights.quote: the average value on that pack when you open it is about 102 103 cents on the dollar

insights.claim: Because graded inventory is scarce, Monster offers permanent buyback offers that scale with card value — 87% of fair market value on $50 packs up to 95-96% on high-end cards — to retain liquidity and inventory.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Buyback spreads reveal where liquidity is tightest (high-end cards command near-par buybacks), a signal of where supply constraints are most acute.
insights.quote: for our $50 packs, it's 87% of fair market value... for our really high-end cards, we offer um 95% buyback

themes.theme: Collectibles as an Emerging Asset Class

insights.claim: The boom is not Pokémon-specific: One Piece and sports cards are also surging, with Fanatics auctions showing multiple million-dollar bids and a Victor Wembanyama one-of-one rookie card selling for over $5 million about a week before recording.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Treat the move as a broad collectibles/alternative-asset rotation rather than a single-IP fad when sizing exposure or fading the trade.
insights.quote: there's like a Victoryama card right now that's, you know, that sold for like a little over $5 million about a week ago

insights.claim: The core power-spender demographic is mid-30s to early-40s collectors who grew up with Pokémon and now have disposable income, normalizing five-figure card purchases as an alternative asset alongside an all-time-high S&P 500.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Demand is tied to a specific aging cohort's wealth; the long-term bull case hinges on whether today's 20-year-olds inherit the same attachment.
insights.quote: it feels like you're not insane to spend five grand on a Charizard because you love Charizard

insights.claim: The boom persisting through three years of 4-5% interest rates (rather than collapsing post-COVID liquidity) signals collectibles maturing into a durable asset class rather than a pure stimulus-driven mania.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Demand resilient to high rates undercuts the simple 'it's just easy money' fade thesis and forces a structural-demand explanation.
insights.quote: we've had high interest rates. We've had sustained like four or five% or whatever for like three years now. And so this seems to be like this is breaking my brain

insights.claim: Brand stewardship of scarcity is a key value driver: Pokémon preserves the value of original rare cards (e.g. base-set Charizard) by only reprinting with obvious twists, whereas Yu-Gi-Oh eroded value by repeatedly reprinting identical cards.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: IP-holder reprint discipline is a fundamental to underwrite — brands that respect the 'social contract' on scarcity sustain collectible value; those that dilute it destroy it.
insights.quote: they would just like reprint the same cards over and over again just kind of like hurt the the value of the original stuff

insights.claim: Pokémon's 30th-anniversary reprints have risen rather than fallen in value despite increasing supply, because nostalgia-driven excitement outweighs dilution — the IP holder effectively 'pumping their bags.'
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive supply/demand: reprints from a strong brand can be catalysts not headwinds, so don't mechanically short on reprint announcements.
insights.quote: you would think that their value would go down because they're being reprinted, but actually their value has gone up because people are excited about these cards

insights.claim: One Piece is the second-largest TCG and has gone 'parabolic' over two years, with some cards pulling genuine 100x moves from ~$1,000 to ~$100,000 despite cards only launching around 2021-2022.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: One Piece is the higher-beta 'altcoin' of the space (host's Pokémon=Bitcoin / One Piece=Ethereum analogy) with bigger upside and downside; a Netflix live-action series is a forward catalyst.
insights.quote: some of their most valuable cards over the last year have pulled like genuine 100 X's going from selling for like $1,000 to $100,000

insights.claim: Continued real-world catalysts (Pokémon's 30th anniversary, the hit game Poctopia, a One Piece Netflix live-action series) keep providing demand triggers, though a post-anniversary lull in demand is plausible.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Map the catalyst calendar; the window after Pokémon 30 launches is a candidate for a demand air-pocket to watch or fade.
insights.quote: maybe after Pokemon 30 comes out and that whole whole bit comes to a close there could be a lull in demand

insights.claim: Three grading firms (PSA, Beckett, CGC) hold a near-monopoly, with PSA owning Beckett, and they monetize aggressively — charging a flat fee then demanding more money to return a card that grades high enough to be valuable.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Grading is a rent-extracting chokepoint and a structural cost/friction; any platform or tech that disintermediates grading captures real value.
insights.quote: they charge you a flat fee and then if it gets graded high enough where the value is higher they say if you want your card back you have to pay more

insights.claim: A grading bottleneck has created a liquidity crunch: PSA effectively stopped taking low-tier submissions and now charges ~$100 per card, with grading taking 6+ months, throttling new graded supply even as Pokémon keeps printing cards.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Constrained graded-card supply supports prices and a sellers' market; a future easing of grading capacity could release latent supply and pressure prices.
insights.quote: it was taking six plus months to get a card graded

insights.claim: Modern cards have matched or outperformed vintage cards partly because owners cannot get newly-valuable modern cards graded fast enough to sell, suppressing realized supply.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: The naive 'vintage outperforms' assumption is wrong in this cycle; grading friction is an under-appreciated driver of modern-card outperformance.
insights.quote: naively you would think the cards that are going to perform the best... are going to be like super vintage cards... But they've been equally, if not outperformed by a lot of more modern cards

insights.claim: Price discovery is opaque: data aggregators (pricecharting.com via eBay, Card Ladder via auction houses Fanatics, Golden, Alt) miss the large volume of offline cash trades at card shows, leaving the market a 'black box.'
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Reported prices understate true volume and are manipulable; treat quoted 'fair market values' with skepticism when underwriting.
insights.quote: most of the market does move offline still. there's a really large volume of trades that happen at shows, people paying cash

insights.claim: The opaque market is currently a sellers' market and is manipulable — actors can buy 50 copies of a card at shows, bid it up 2-3x at auction houses to mark paper gains, then offload offline.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Thin, opaque liquidity means quoted appreciation can be partly engineered; beware wash-trading-style price marks when valuing collections.
insights.quote: people who will go to a bunch of shows and buy 50 copies of the same card and then go to the auction houses and bid up that card two or 3x

insights.claim: Gotcha/repack platforms are a 'Trojan horse' quietly tokenizing millions of dollars of physical cards per week (Courtyard, Collector Crypt, Figitals, Bezy all issue cards as NFTs), gradually moving the collectibles market on-chain.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: On-chain card volume is an emerging real-world-asset tokenization vertical; track it as a 'canary' for renewed NFT/RWA demand.
insights.quote: they are buying millions and millions of dollars of cards a week and they are tokenizing all of them and putting them in these packs

insights.claim: On-chain card marketplaces (resembling early OpenSea, with aggregators like Only Slabs and Traded) enable strategies like buying cards listed below fair market value and redeeming them, but pack-opening still drives ~99% of volume.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: The secondary on-chain marketplace is nascent (pack-opening is the foundation); arbitrage exists for those monitoring below-FMV listings.
insights.quote: the the like pack opening stuff is really the main draw and does like 99% of the volume

insights.claim: Stablecoin payments may be the bigger on-chain unlock than tokenization because trading-card commerce suffers heavy fraud and chargebacks, and in-person shows run on cash only.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Stablecoin rails address a concrete pain point (chargeback fraud, cash-only shows) — a clearer near-term crypto use case than collectible tokenization itself.
insights.quote: the stable coin payment side of it is massive because there's a lot of fraud in these markets

insights.claim: Tokenized cards on these platforms carry custodial risk because issuance is second-party (the platforms, not the IP holders) and users must trust the platform's custody of the underlying physical card.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: On-chain collectibles are not trustless RWAs; platform solvency and custody integrity are the key risks to underwrite before holding these tokens.
insights.quote: it's all second party you still have to have custody you so there's there's still some custodial risk

insights.claim: Platforms use auction-house vaulting services located in no-sales-tax states so buyers avoid sales tax, with redemptions shipped directly from vaults — a tax-driven mechanism that also locks users into ecosystems.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Sales-tax arbitrage via no-tax-state vaults is a structural reason inventory stays vaulted/on-platform — a moat and a regulatory dependency to monitor.
insights.quote: if you basically like buy and ship to their vaults, you don't have to pay sales tax on the purchase

insights.claim: The platform space is fragmented and primed for consolidation (likened to the early-crypto era of ~17 B/C-tier exchanges), with survivors differentiated by region, card type, or distribution.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Expect winner-take-most dynamics; platforms without a unique distribution or user-base angle are consolidation targets.
insights.quote: if you don't have some sort of unique distribution angle or way to some some unique user base, there will definitely be consolidation among the the top players

insights.claim: The guest believes the market may be at a local top for Pokémon/One Piece demand, citing NFT-style mania signals like card-show vendor tables selling out within 30 minutes.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: An insider flags froth and personal NFT-crash 'PTSD'; a tactical caution signal even within a structurally bullish long-term view.
insights.quote: I think that we very well could be at a local top for the demand for Pokemon One Piece these things

insights.claim: The primary long-term risk to Pokémon as an asset is generational: whether today's 20-year-olds will still want to buy when they reach mid-30s with disposable income, since current demand rests on 30-40-year-olds' childhood nostalgia.
insights.speaker: Andy 8052, guest, Monster founder
insights.so_what: Nostalgia-anchored demand is cohort-dependent; ongoing kid engagement (new games like Poctopia) is the key forward metric for multi-decade durability.
insights.quote: currently 30 to 40 year olds love Pokemon... people who are 20 right now when they're 35 and they have disposable income, are they going to love it?
